contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC Asia Jakarta Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Read the full definition, samples, and N,M <= 300000 constraints.
Read the official Jakarta analysis for I on page 10; confirmed the filtered-run interpretation and Fenwick sweep over active last occurrences.
Read the Ling Cha Shan Ai Fu solution; confirmed the cleaner per-occurrence formula: M-1 for a first occurrence and a last-position range count for every repeat.
Re-derived the contribution bijection and matched the sweep against explicit f(x,y) computation on random arrays through length 9 and M through 6.

annotation.summary: Sum, over every value pair 1 <= x < y <= M, the maximum length of a subsequence using only x and y with unequal adjacent elements.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= N,M <= 300000 and 1 <= A_i <= M. Enumerating O(M^2) value pairs is impossible; the answer and accumulated contributions require 64-bit integers.
annotation.core_idea: For fixed x,y, filter A to those two values. The longest valid subsequence takes one element from each maximal equal-value run, so f(x,y) is exactly the number of runs in the filtered sequence. Count all such runs by sweeping A. Keep last[v] and a Fenwick tree with a 1 only at the latest position of each value seen so far. At the first occurrence of x, add M-1: for every y!=x this occurrence supplies either the first run, the transition after an earlier y, or the sole run when y never occurs. At a repeated x at position i with previous position p=last[x], it starts one new run for pair (x,y) exactly when y occurs between p and i; this is equivalent to the latest occurrence of y lying in [p+1,i-1]. Query that interval in the Fenwick tree, remove the marker at p, add one at i, and update last[x].
annotation.complexity: O(M + N log N) time and O(M + N) memory.
tricks: replace each longest alternating two-value subsequence by the run count of its filtered sequence
charge the total answer to individual occurrences
represent every seen value by only its latest position
turn distinct values in a range into a Fenwick range sum over active last occurrences

solution_variants.name: last-occurrence-contribution-fenwick
solution_variants.summary: Sweep left to right. Charge M-1 for each value's first occurrence; for a repeat, count active latest occurrences strictly after its previous copy with a Fenwick tree, then move that value's active marker to the current index.
solution_variants.complexity: O(M + N log N) time and O(M + N) memory.

tags.evidence: The O(M^2) sum is inverted into per-occurrence contributions: M-1 at first appearances and one contribution for each active value whose latest occurrence lies after the previous equal element.
A Fenwick tree maintains one marker at each value's latest position and answers the decisive count of distinct active values in (last[x],i) in O(log N).
Whether the current x extends the alternating sequence for pair (x,y) depends only on whether y's latest occurrence is newer than the previous x; older occurrences are irrelevant.
